title: Repowise Central Codebase Intelligence System — Install, Configure, Integrate

stress_test.st1: BROADEST reading — this is NOT 'just pip install repowise'. It's a full infrastructure deployment: Python version upgrade, Ollama model pulling, Repowise install + init + configure + serve, MCP integration with Claude Code, CLAUDE.md generation with preserved sections, user journey documentation, daily maintenance workflow, AND validation that all 4 pain points are actually addressed. Plus it must work across ALL of the user's projects going forward — not just this one repo.
stress_test.st2: Risk is 'too narrow' — easy to stop at 'repowise is installed' and declare victory. The real test is: does the LLM actually use it? Are token savings measurable? Does the CLAUDE.md stay current? Do journey optimizations actually work?
stress_test.st3: A frustrated user would say: 'You installed it but my Python is 3.10 and it needs 3.11 — did you even check? And you didn't verify the MCP tools actually respond. And where's the user journey documentation layer?'
stress_test.st4: 7 sources: (1) Repowise GitHub repo + docs, (2) PyPI version constraints, (3) user's local Python/Ollama/conda environment, (4) this project's codebase structure, (5) Claude Code MCP config mechanism, (6) GitHub issues for known bugs, (7) alternative tools (codebase-memory-mcp). Must extract from ALL independently.
stress_test.st5: Implicit requirements: Python 3.11+ upgrade path (hard blocker — user has 3.10.11), Ollama embedding model pull (nomic-embed-text), backup of existing CLAUDE.md before Repowise overwrites it, .gitignore update for .repowise/, validation that get_why() doesn't hang (known 50% failure rate), confidence score calibration (search_codebase returns 1.0 always — broken), Windows path handling, scalability to other projects (not just this repo).
stress_test.st6: YES — the hard parts are: (a) Python upgrade without breaking existing tools, (b) verifying MCP tools actually work end-to-end with Claude Code CLI (untested by Repowise team), (c) building the user journey documentation layer that Repowise doesn't auto-generate, (d) establishing a maintenance workflow that doesn't drift. Easy to skip these and just document the install steps.

sources: Repowise GitHub repo (README, QUICKSTART, CONFIG.md, critical-analysis.md)
PyPI package metadata (Requires-Python >=3.11, pure Python wheel)
User's local environment (Python 3.10.11, Ollama 0.20.3, conda 24.11.3, 219 git commits)
Claude Code MCP config (~/.claude/.mcp.json or claude_desktop_config.json)
GitHub issues (3 open for Repowise, 32 open for codebase-memory-mcp)
codebase-memory-mcp (MIT, single binary, 14 MCP tools, Windows binary available)
dimensions: Prerequisites & blockers (Python version, Ollama models, disk space)
Installation & configuration (pip install, init, config.yaml, serve)
MCP integration (Claude Code CLI config, tool verification, pre-tool hooks)
CLAUDE.md generation (preserved sections, architecture, hotspots, decisions)
User journey documentation (manual layer, decisions, Mermaid diagrams)
Validation & smoke testing (all 8 MCP tools, known broken tools, fallback strategy)
Maintenance workflow (update, watch, doctor, decision health, dead-code)
Scalability (template for other projects, reusable config, documented process)

anti_bias_analysis.rewritten_prompt: DEPLOY a complete Repowise-based codebase intelligence system for this project (C:/Users/anujd/Documents/01_AI/214_Chat_overlay_widget) and establish a reusable template for all future projects. PREREQUISITES: Upgrade Python from 3.10.11 to 3.11+ (required by Repowise >=3.11). Pull Ollama embedding model. INSTALL: pip install repowise, repowise doctor, repowise init --index-only (free mode first). CONFIGURE: config.yaml for Ollama provider, nomic-embed-text embeddings, git intelligence, dead code detection. INTEGRATE: Configure Claude Code CLI MCP server, verify all 8 tools respond correctly, document known broken tools (get_why hangs 50%, search_codebase confidence=1.0 always). GENERATE: repowise generate-claude-md with preserved user section containing end-user journey documentation, Mermaid workflow diagrams, LLM instruction block. VALIDATE: Smoke test every MCP tool, measure token reduction on a representative query, confirm CLAUDE.md accuracy. MAINTAIN: Establish daily workflow (update, watch, doctor, decision health). SCALE: Document the process as a reusable template for other projects. CONSTRAINT: Zero breakage to existing code, functions, or features. Brownfield approach only.

evidence.mcp_config_file: Project .mcp.json updated with correct config: absolute path to C:/Users/anujd/miniconda3/envs/repowise/Scripts/repowise.exe, positional path arg (not --path), PYTHONUTF8=1 env var

Fixed in project .mcp.json to use absolute exe path and correct positional arg format. settings.json entry left untouched (cannot modify per rules) — project .mcp.json takes precedence for this project.

known_broken_tools.search_codebase: PARTIALLY BROKEN — confidence_score always returns 1.0 (normalization bug: raw/max_score). Workaround: use relevance_score for ranking, use get_context() for known targets

evidence.manual_steps_required: User should add MCP_TOOL_TIMEOUT=15000 and MCP_TIMEOUT=30000 to ~/.claude/settings.json env section to convert get_why() hangs to recoverable timeouts. Cannot be done by builder agent per settings.json modification rules.

Tested conceptually: replacing <PROJECT_PATH> with any absolute Windows path (e.g., C:\Users\anujd\Documents\01_AI\215_Another_Project) produces valid commands for a second project.

research_findings.critical_blocker: Python 3.10.11 installed, Repowise requires >=3.11. pip install will fail without upgrade.
research_findings.repowise_maturity: 11 days old (v0.2.1, released March 28, 2026). AGPL-3.0. 3 open issues (all feature requests). Zero public bug reports despite internal critical-analysis.md documenting: silent data loss risk, get_why() 50% hang rate, search_codebase confidence=1.0 always, NetworkX crash >100K files.

known_broken_tools.get_why: Hangs 50% of test runs, returns irrelevant results when it works

research_findings.alternative_considered: codebase-memory-mcp (MIT, single binary, 14 MCP tools, Windows binary). Pros: no Python dep, 10x token reduction (academic paper), faster indexing. Cons: 32 open issues, silent failures on TypeScript files (issue #213), no CLAUDE.md generation, Windows install issue (#221). Decision: proceed with Repowise as primary (matches user's explicit request), document codebase-memory-mcp as fallback/complement.
research_findings.windows_status: Repowise: untested on Windows but pure Python wheel (no C extensions). codebase-memory-mcp: Windows binary exists but install script has open issue #221.
research_findings.ollama_status: Ollama 0.20.3 installed on user's machine. nomic-embed-text model needs to be pulled. Repowise config supports provider: ollama natively.

risk_mitigations._methodology: Each risk was researched with 4-7 candidate options. Each option was stress-tested from adversarial views (compatibility, reversibility, maintenance burden, failure modes). Winners selected based on documented evidence, not assumptions.

winning_solution.strategy: conda create -n repowise python=3.11 — isolated environment
winning_solution.evidence: conda docs explicitly state 'upgrading Python in base is not recommended; create a new environment instead'. Isolated env leaves C:\Python310 untouched — zero risk to hooks, node-gyp, or existing tools.
implementation: conda create -n repowise python=3.11 -y
conda activate repowise && pip install repowise==0.2.1
Set NODE_GYP_FORCE_PYTHON=C:\Python310\python.exe as env var to prevent node-gyp confusion
MCP config uses absolute path: C:\Users\anujd\miniconda3\envs\repowise\Scripts\repowise.exe

stress_tests.adversarial_view_1_hooks_breakage: SAFE — hooks run under base Python (C:\Python310), conda env is completely isolated. Verified: conda activate does NOT change system PATH for other processes.
stress_tests.adversarial_view_2_node_gyp: SAFE — node-gyp uses NODE_GYP_FORCE_PYTHON env var which bypasses all PATH search. Pin to C:\Python310\python.exe explicitly.
stress_tests.adversarial_view_3_mcp_launch: SAFE — Claude Code launches MCP servers via absolute path to executable, not via shell activation. Use full path to conda env's repowise.exe. Confirmed working pattern from community configs.
stress_tests.adversarial_view_4_maintenance: LOW BURDEN — conda env is persistent. repowise.exe path is stable. Only risk: conda update could theoretically break the env, mitigated by pinning repowise==0.2.1.

winning_solution.rollback: conda env remove -n repowise — complete cleanup, zero residue

rejected_options.R1-C_reason: conda docs explicitly warn against upgrading base Python. Real-world GitHub issues confirm 45+ minute dependency resolution that often fails with conflicts. psycopg2 would need reinstall from conda-forge. High disruption, zero benefit over R1-A.
rejected_options.R1-B_reason: Viable but adds PATH management complexity. Two system Pythons on Windows cause subtle confusion for scripts calling bare 'python'. More fragile than conda env isolation.
rejected_options.R1-D_reason: Viable but adds pipx as an additional dependency layer. pipx binary location on Windows is less predictable (~/.local/bin/ vs appdata). Conda is already installed.

risks.risk: get_why() MCP tool hangs 50% of the time

options_evaluated.option: Skip get_why(), use get_context() + CLAUDE.md decisions as alternative

winning_solution.strategy: Three-layer defense: (1) MCP_TOOL_TIMEOUT=15000ms converts hangs to recoverable errors, (2) OLLAMA_KEEP_ALIVE=-1 eliminates cold-start hangs, (3) treat get_why() as optional — use get_context() + preserved CLAUDE.md decisions as primary decision-lookup path
winning_solution.evidence: MCP_TOOL_TIMEOUT confirmed working in Claude Code issue #5615. OLLAMA_KEEP_ALIVE=-1 documented in Ollama docs and confirmed in ollama-python issues #218/#483 for preventing model unloading between calls. get_why() returns 'irrelevant results when it works' per internal analysis — quality floor is already low, so skipping it loses minimal value.
implementation: Add to ~/.claude/settings.json: {"env": {"MCP_TOOL_TIMEOUT": "15000", "MCP_TIMEOUT": "30000"}}
Set system env var: OLLAMA_KEEP_ALIVE=-1 (or add to Ollama config)
Add to CLAUDE.md LLM instruction block: 'Use get_context() + get_risk() for code understanding. get_why() is unreliable — prefer reading Decisions in CLAUDE.md preserved section.'
Document in maintenance template: get_why() is known-broken as of v0.2.1

stress_tests.adversarial_view_1_timeout_too_short: 15s is generous — legitimate get_context() calls return in <2s on local Ollama. If get_why() needs >15s, it's already in a hang state. Adjustable later.
stress_tests.adversarial_view_2_global_timeout_side_e: MCP_TOOL_TIMEOUT is global (applies to ALL MCP tools, not just Repowise). Other MCP servers (aidesigner, chat-overlay) may be affected. 15s should be sufficient for all local tools. Monitor.
stress_tests.adversarial_view_3_losing_why_context: get_why() synthesizes from git history + ADRs. This CAN be replicated: store decisions in CLAUDE.md preserved section + use get_context() for code locations + git log for history. Net loss: automated synthesis convenience only.
stress_tests.adversarial_view_4_ollama_keep_alive_mem: OLLAMA_KEEP_ALIVE=-1 keeps model in VRAM permanently. qwen3:8b uses ~5GB VRAM. If user runs GPU-heavy tasks (Tauri compilation, other models), this could cause OOM. Mitigation: monitor VRAM usage, can revert to default keep-alive if needed.

winning_solution.rollback: Remove MCP_TOOL_TIMEOUT from settings.json, unset OLLAMA_KEEP_ALIVE

rejected_options.R2-C_reason: No fixed version exists. v0.2.1 is the only release. Bug present since initial release.
rejected_options.R2-D_reason: No config.yaml option to disable LLM synthesis for get_why(). --index-only only affects indexing, not query-time behavior.

risks.risk: search_codebase() confidence scores always return 1.0 (broken calibration)

options_evaluated.option: Use relevance_score instead of confidence_score, pair with git log for staleness
Use get_context(targets) as alternative (no vector search)
Wait for fix in newer version
File GitHub issue with root cause

winning_solution.strategy: Use relevance_score (not confidence_score) for ranking results. Cross-reference with git log for staleness detection. Use get_context() for known-target lookups (no vector search involved). File issue with identified root cause.
winning_solution.evidence: Root cause identified in tool_search.py: confidence_score = raw/max_score, so top result is always 1.0 by mathematical necessity. The bug is in the normalization formula, not the embedding model. relevance_score is the raw LanceDB distance — more meaningful for ranking. get_context() takes explicit targets and bypasses vector search entirely.
implementation: Add to LLM instruction block: 'When using search_codebase, ignore confidence_score (known broken). Use relevance_score for ranking. For known targets, prefer get_context() over search_codebase().'
Add git log --since cross-reference to search workflow for staleness validation
File GitHub issue at repowise-dev/repowise with root cause analysis (tool_search.py normalization formula)

stress_tests.adversarial_view_1_relevance_score_meani: LanceDB L2 distances cluster tightly for high-dimensional embeddings (concentration of measure). Ranking is still useful even if absolute values aren't interpretable. Better than confidence_score which is mathematically guaranteed to be 1.0 for top result.
stress_tests.adversarial_view_2_no_staleness_detectio: Without working confidence scores, staleness detection falls to git log. This is actually MORE reliable — git log shows real file modification dates, while Repowise's git-freshness boost is already baked into relevance_score.
stress_tests.adversarial_view_3_get_context_not_seman: get_context() requires you to know the target path/name. For 'find code related to X' queries, search_codebase is still needed. Acceptable tradeoff: search_codebase results are still ranked correctly by relevance_score, just confidence is meaningless.

winning_solution.rollback: No rollback needed — purely additive documentation/instruction changes

rejected_options.R3-D_reason: Bug is in normalization formula (raw/max_score), not embedding model. Any model produces the same broken ratio for top result.

risks.risk: Alpha software (11 days old) — silent data loss, crash-on-checkpoint, SQL/vector desync

options_evaluated.option: Use --index-only mode (avoids LLM write path where desync occurs)
Run repowise doctor --repair after every init/update
Treat .repowise/ as ephemeral (rebuild from scratch on corruption)

winning_solution.strategy: Triple defense: (1) --index-only mode eliminates the LLM write path where 5-15% desync risk lives, (2) treat .repowise/ as ephemeral — if corrupted, rm and re-init in <2 minutes for a 500-file project, (3) pin repowise==0.2.1 to prevent silent upgrade breakage
winning_solution.evidence: --index-only is documented in QUICKSTART as skipping LLM calls during indexing. Graph + git intelligence + dead code all work without LLM. The documented desync risk is in 'large init + LLM write path' — removing LLM calls removes the desync vector. Re-init time for comparable tools (tree-sitter indexers) on 500 files: 15-60 seconds. NetworkX crash threshold is >100K files — this project has ~500 files, well within safe range.
implementation: Always use: repowise init --index-only (never run without --index-only until tool matures)
Pin: pip install repowise==0.2.1 (prevent silent upgrades)
Rollback procedure: rm -rf .repowise/ && repowise init --index-only (documented as official recovery)
Optional: repowise doctor as secondary check (not primary safety mechanism)
Do NOT rely on doctor --repair for data integrity — zero community evidence it works

stress_tests.adversarial_view_1_losing_wiki_generatio: Wiki auto-generation requires LLM calls, which we're skipping with --index-only. Loss: no auto-generated prose summaries per module. Mitigation: CLAUDE.md generation (separate command) still works. Manual journey docs fill the gap. Graph + git intelligence (the core value) are fully preserved.
stress_tests.adversarial_view_2_rebuild_time: For 500 files with 219 commits, re-init should be <2 minutes. Even if it takes 5 minutes, this is acceptable for a corruption recovery that should be rare. The alternative (attempting repair on a corrupted SQLite/vector store) is riskier.
stress_tests.adversarial_view_3_losing_decisions: Decisions added via `repowise decision add` are stored in .repowise/. If we rm -rf, they're lost. Mitigation: also store decisions in CLAUDE.md preserved section and/or docs/user-journeys.md (version-controlled, survives .repowise/ deletion).
stress_tests.adversarial_view_4_agpl_license: NON-ISSUE for local developer tooling. AGPL network clause only triggers for networked services others interact with. Repowise runs locally, never exposed over network. Does not link into Tauri app binary. No license contamination risk.

winning_solution.rollback: rm -rf .repowise/ — complete cleanup. Decisions preserved in CLAUDE.md/docs/

rejected_options.R4-C_reason: Zero community evidence that doctor --repair actually fixes SQL/vector/FTS desync. No public documentation of what repair operations it performs. Using it as primary safety mechanism would be unverified trust in alpha software.

risks.risk: Windows 11 compatibility — untested by Repowise team

options_evaluated.option: Pre-validate dependencies (LanceDB, Tree-sitter, watchdog) on Windows
Test in isolated venv on throwaway directory first
PowerShell path handling (forward slashes in config)
Fallback to Task Scheduler if watchdog fails

winning_solution.strategy: Pre-validated + test-first approach: (1) All critical deps have documented Windows support (LanceDB blog post, tree-sitter pre-built wheels, watchdog ReadDirectoryChangesW), (2) Test on throwaway directory before real project, (3) Use forward slashes in all config files, (4) Enable Windows long paths if needed
winning_solution.evidence: LanceDB: Official Windows support blog post 'Lance, Windows. Windows, Lance.' Pre-built wheels from v0.4.0+. Only risk: exFAT drives (user is on NTFS — confirmed safe). Tree-sitter: Pre-compiled binary wheels ship for Windows x64. No MSVC needed for pip install. watchdog 6.0.0: Uses ReadDirectoryChangesW (native Windows kernel API), not a polled fallback. First-class Windows implementation.
implementation: Phase 3 step 0: Create throwaway test directory, run repowise init --index-only on it first
Verify: .repowise/ created, no errors, SQLite store accessible
Only then point at real project directory
Use forward slashes in config.yaml and MCP config (Python pathlib normalizes both)
If watchdog fails: fall back to Task Scheduler running 'repowise update' every 15 minutes
If MAX_PATH hit: enable long paths via registry (documented Microsoft fix)

stress_tests.adversarial_view_1_lancedb_untested: LanceDB has an official Windows blog post and ships pre-built wheels. Only documented failure is exFAT (GitHub issue #1231). User's C:\ is NTFS. Risk: LOW.
stress_tests.adversarial_view_2_watchdog_quirks: Known behavior: rename/move events may fire before I/O completes on Windows. For repowise watch on a stable codebase (edits, not bulk renames), this is a non-issue. Worst case: a file change is missed, caught on next manual update.
stress_tests.adversarial_view_3_path_spaces: User's path contains spaces: '01_AI' is fine but 'Documents' is a standard Windows path. Python pathlib handles this. YAML string values handle spaces without quoting. PowerShell CLI commands need double-quote wrapping for paths with spaces.
stress_tests.adversarial_view_4_max_path: Project path is ~60 chars. .repowise/ internal paths add ~40 more. Total ~100 chars. Windows MAX_PATH is 260. No risk for this project. Larger projects with deep nesting could hit it — long paths registry fix is the documented mitigation.

winning_solution.rollback: Throwaway test catches failures before touching real project. If real project fails: rm -rf .repowise/

rejected_options.R5-B_reason: Project constraints explicitly state: 'no WSL'. Ruled out.

risks.risk: CLAUDE.md overwrite — existing ~400-line project-specific content could be destroyed

options_evaluated.option: Pre-insert REPOWISE markers before running generate-claude-md

winning_solution.strategy: Three-step safe merge: (1) Preview with --stdout first, (2) Pre-insert REPOWISE:START/END markers at bottom of existing CLAUDE.md, (3) Run generate-claude-md — it fills between markers, never touches content outside
winning_solution.evidence: Repowise docs/EDITOR_FILES.md documents the three-case merge table: file without markers → appends at bottom (preserves all existing). File with markers → replaces only between markers. The --stdout flag is confirmed in docs/USER_GUIDE.md. Additional safety: repowise init --no-claude-md skips generation entirely if you want to defer.
implementation: Step 1: cp CLAUDE.md CLAUDE.md.backup (safety net)
Step 2: repowise generate-claude-md --stdout (preview output, zero risk)
Step 3: Manually append markers to bottom of CLAUDE.md:
<!-- REPOWISE:START — Do not edit below this line. Auto-generated by Repowise. -->
<!-- REPOWISE:END -->
Step 4: repowise generate-claude-md (fills between markers only)
Step 5: Verify all original sections (Project, Technology Stack, GSD Workflow, etc.) are intact
Step 6: Add LLM instruction block to preserved section at top

stress_tests.adversarial_view_1_marker_format_wrong: Marker format is documented in EDITOR_FILES.md. If format doesn't match exactly, Repowise falls back to 'file exists without markers' behavior — which APPENDS at bottom, still preserving existing content. Fail-safe by design.
stress_tests.adversarial_view_2_subsequent_updates: On every repowise update, only content between markers is refreshed. Preserved sections are never touched. This is the documented, intended workflow.
stress_tests.adversarial_view_3_backup_redundant: Even though Repowise preserves content, the backup costs nothing and provides absolute safety. Keep it until trust is established after 2-3 successful updates.
stress_tests.adversarial_view_4_separate_file: REJECTED: Repowise MCP tools are hardcoded to read CLAUDE.md. A separate REPOWISE.md would not be discovered by get_overview() etc. Must be in the same file.

winning_solution.rollback: cp CLAUDE.md.backup CLAUDE.md — instant restore from backup

rejected_options.R6-C_reason: --dry-run flag does not exist in the CLI. Use --stdout instead (confirmed in USER_GUIDE.md).
rejected_options.R6-E_reason: Repowise MCP tools are hardcoded to expect CLAUDE.md. A separate file would not be indexed or queryable via MCP tools.

risks.risk: MCP integration untested with Claude Code CLI (only documented for Claude Desktop)

options_evaluated.option: Use project-level .mcp.json with absolute path to conda env repowise.exe
Use claude mcp add command for auto-configuration

winning_solution.strategy: Use claude mcp add -s project for project-scoped config (better for scalability), with absolute path to conda env's repowise.exe. Verify with claude mcp list. Format is identical between CLI and Desktop (same mcpServers key).
winning_solution.evidence: User's existing ~/.claude/mcp.json and project .mcp.json both confirmed working with identical format. claude mcp add/list/get/remove commands all confirmed present in Claude Code CLI. MCP config is structurally identical between CLI and Desktop — same mcpServers key, same command/args structure. Community configs confirm Windows absolute paths work (e.g., D:\path\to\node.exe).
implementation: claude mcp add -s project repowise C:\Users\anujd\miniconda3\envs\repowise\Scripts\repowise.exe -- mcp --path C:\Users\anujd\Documents\01_AI\214_Chat_overlay_widget
OR manually add to project .mcp.json:

{"mcpServers": {"repowise": {"command": "C:\\Users\\anujd\\miniconda3\\envs\\repowise\\Scripts\\repowise.exe", "args": ["mcp", "--path", "C:\\Users\\anujd\\Documents\\01_AI\\214_Chat_overlay_widget"]}}}
Verify: claude mcp list (should show repowise as Connected)
Smoke test: restart Claude Code, ask it to call get_overview()

stress_tests.adversarial_view_1_stdio_windows_pipes: Claude Code launches MCP servers via child_process with stdio transport. Windows named pipes work differently than Unix. Risk: stdio buffer issues on large outputs. Mitigation: Repowise responses are JSON, typically <10KB. If stdio fails, HTTP transport is available as fallback.
stress_tests.adversarial_view_2_project_scope_portabi: Project-scoped .mcp.json means other devs cloning the repo would see it. The absolute path to conda env is machine-specific. Mitigation: add .mcp.json to .gitignore if not already there. Or use user-scoped config.
stress_tests.adversarial_view_3_conda_env_activation: Claude Code does NOT activate conda envs — it launches the executable directly. Using absolute path to repowise.exe in the conda env bypasses activation entirely. The executable is self-contained within the env's Scripts/ directory.
stress_tests.adversarial_view_4_multiple_projects: For scalability: each project needs its own MCP config with --path pointing to that project. Project-scoped .mcp.json handles this naturally. Template the config in docs/repowise-setup-template.md.

winning_solution.rollback: claude mcp remove repowise — or delete the entry from .mcp.json

rejected_options.R7-D_reason: HTTP transport requires repowise serve running continuously. Stdio is simpler (auto-launched by Claude Code on demand). Defer HTTP to fallback if stdio proves unstable on Windows.
